does: This paper develops a unified theoretical framework for understanding sparse dictionary learning methods used in mechanistic interpretability, including sparse autoencoders, transcoders, and crosscoders. The authors analyze these methods through the lens of piecewise biconvex optimization, characterizing their loss landscapes and identifying why practical training produces polysemantic features, feature absorption, and dead neurons. By studying the optimization geometry of SDL objectives with sparsity constraints, the work provides formal theoretical grounding for phenomena that were previously only empirically observed, connecting optimization difficulty to feature quality degradation.
connects: This work extends prior theoretical analyses that focused narrowly on tied-weight sparse autoencoders to encompass the broader family of SDL methods now central to mechanistic interpretability research. It builds on empirical observations from recent work showing that neural networks represent concepts as linear directions and encode multiple concepts in superposition, which motivated the development of SDL techniques. The paper also connects to classical sparse coding and dictionary learning theory from signal processing, adapting those frameworks to the specific challenges of interpreting neural network representations and addressing the gap between existing theory and the practical performance limitations observed across SDL variants.
enables: This theoretical foundation enables principled improvements to sparse dictionary learning methods by identifying optimization-level sources of feature quality problems. Understanding piecewise biconvexity and spurious minima landscapes can guide the design of better training procedures, initialization schemes, and regularization strategies that avoid polysemanticity. The framework also enables more rigorous evaluation of SDL methods and helps interpret why certain design choices succeed or fail. Beyond mechanistic interpretability specifically, connecting sparse dictionary learning to biconvex optimization theory opens directions for developing stronger convergence guarantees and potentially new algorithmic approaches for training these models more effectively.
